audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Gerasene release, Legion naming, return-home witness, Jairus, hemorrhaging woman, sozo as saved/healed, daughter language, fear/trust, and raising of Jairus' daughter.

verses.restored: They came to the other side of the sea, into the region of the Gerasenes.
Immediately, as he came out of the boat, a man in an unclean spirit met him from the tombs.
He had his dwelling among the tombs, and no one was able to bind him anymore, not even with a chain.
For he had often been bound with shackles and chains, but the chains were torn apart by him and the shackles crushed; no one was strong enough to tame him.
Night and day among the tombs and mountains he was crying out and cutting himself with stones.
Seeing Jesus from afar, he ran and bowed/prostrated before him.
Crying out with a great voice, he said, What to me and to you, Jesus, Son of the Most High Divine? I adjure you by the Divine: do not torment me.
For Jesus had been saying to him, Come out from the man, unclean spirit.
Jesus asked him, What is your name? He said to him, Legion is my name, because we are many.
He pleaded with him many times not to send them out of the region.
Now there was there on the mountain a great herd of pigs feeding.
They pleaded with him, saying, Send us into the pigs, so that we may enter them.
He permitted them. The unclean spirits came out and entered the pigs; the herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea, about two thousand, and were drowned in the sea.
Those feeding them fled and reported it in the city and in the fields; and people came to see what had happened.
They came to Jesus and saw the man who had been troubled by Legion sitting, clothed, and sound-minded; and they were afraid.
Those who had seen explained to them what had happened to the man troubled by unclean spirits, and about the pigs.
They began to plead with Jesus to depart from their borders.
As he was entering the boat, the one who had been troubled pleaded that he might be with him.
He did not permit him, but said, Go to your house, to your own people, and report to them how much the Lord has done for you and how he had mercy on you.
He went away and began proclaiming in the Decapolis how much Jesus had done for him, and everyone marveled.
When Jesus crossed again in the boat to the other side, a great crowd gathered around him, and he was beside the sea.
One of the synagogue rulers came, Jairus by name, and seeing him, fell at his feet.
He pleaded with him many times, saying, My little daughter is at the point of death; come lay hands on her, so that she may be saved/healed and live.
Jesus went with him, and a great crowd followed him and pressed around him.
A woman was there with a flow of blood twelve years.
She had suffered much under many physicians and spent all she had, yet was not helped but rather became worse.
Having heard about Jesus, she came behind him in the crowd and touched his garment.
For she was saying, If I touch even his garments, I will be saved/healed.
Immediately the spring of her blood dried up, and she knew in her body that she had been healed from the affliction.
Immediately Jesus, knowing in himself that power had gone out from him, turned in the crowd and said, Who touched my garments?
His disciples said to him, You see the crowd pressing around you, and you say, Who touched me?
He looked around to see the one who had done this.
The woman, fearing and trembling, knowing what had happened to her, came, fell before him, and told him all the truth.
He said to her, Daughter, your trust has saved/healed you. Go into peace, and be whole from your affliction.
While he was still speaking, some came from the synagogue ruler's house, saying, Your daughter has died. Why trouble the teacher still?
But Jesus, overhearing the word spoken, said to the synagogue ruler, Do not fear; only trust.
He did not allow anyone to follow with him except Peter, James, and John the brother of James.
They came to the house of the synagogue ruler, and he saw a commotion, people weeping and wailing loudly.
Entering, he said to them, Why do you make commotion and weep? The child has not died but sleeps.
They laughed at him. But he put them all outside, took the child's father and mother and those with him, and entered where the child was.
Taking the child's hand, he said to her, Talitha koum, which is translated, Little girl, I say to you, rise.
Immediately the girl rose and walked, for she was twelve years old. Immediately they were overcome with great amazement.
He strictly ordered them that no one should know this, and said that something should be given her to eat.
